title: Step Dependency Questionnaire
instructions: You are presented with a problem and a chain of thought generated by an AI when solving it. For each question, the chain of thought is shown up to a 'target step' (highlighted in yellow). Your task: From the four options provided, select the step that is crucial for the target step. All other steps being equal, the crucial step absence would cause the AI to generate a significantly different target step. Only one of the four options is crucial. The other three, if absent, would not significantly change the target step. A snippet of text below the target step shows how the target step in yellow will begin if the crucial step was indeed omitted. Use this as a hint.

questions.main_question:
Premises:

1. 1. All employees who schedule a meeting with their customers will go to the company building today.
2. 2. Everyone who has lunch in the company building schedules meetings with their customers.
3. 3. Employees have lunch either in the company building or at home.
4. 4. If an employee has lunch at home, they are working remotely from home.
5. 5. All employees who are in other countries work remotely from home.
6. 6. No managers work remotely from home.
7. 7. James will appear in the company today if and only if he is a manager.

Conclusion:

If James is either a manager or in other countries, then James does not either has lunch at home or work remotely from home. If James either has lunch at home or works remotely from home, then he is neither a manager nor does he work in other countries. [contrapositive is more natural]

Determine whether the conclusion is entailed by the premises.
questions.target_step_text: The second premise: "Everyone who has lunch in the company building schedules meetings with their customers." So, if someone has lunch in the company building, they must have scheduled a meeting.
questions.counterfactual_starting_text: So if someone schedules a meeting with their customers, they will go to the company building today. Next, the second premise says that everyone who has lunch in the company building schedules meetings with their

choices.text: Okay, let's try to figure out if the conclusion is entailed by the premises.
First, I need to understand all the premises and then see if the conclusion logically follows from them.
Let me start by breaking down each premise one by one.

The first premise says: "All employees who schedule a meeting with their customers will go to the company building today." So, if an employee schedules a meeting with customers, they must be at the company building today.
